summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the Court is satisfied that the requirements of s 79AA(9)(b) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) are met – Where relationship of moderate length – Where there is one child of the relationship who lives in a shared care arrangement –Where the Respondent Wife owned real property at the commencement of the relationship – Where the Respondent Wife abandoned an argument that the real property was held on trust for her parents – Where the Applicant Husband also owned real property at the commencement of the relationship which was sold – Where the Respondent Wife sought a “two pool” approach with real property in a separate pool – Where the Applicant Husband sought a “one pool” approach – Where “one pool” approach adopted – Where parties’ respective contributions are weighed –Where the Applicant Husband is now unemployed and not paying child support – Where the Respondent Wife has modest income – Where no superannuation split sought – Where no adjustment for current and future financial circumstances is just and equitable – Where orders made for the Applicant Husband to receive 35 per cent and the Respondent Wife to receive 65 per cent of the property

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BEHRENS J

These are financial proceedings under pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) between the applicant husband, Mr Astor, and the respondent wife, Ms Lennon. Following their separation, Mr Astor and Ms Lennon have been able to reach an agreement in respect of their daughter, X, aged six. X lives in an almost equal shared care arrangement between them. The parties have not been able to resolve their financial dispute, which involves a property pool of about $2 million consisting of an apartment at Suburb C, funds in a controlled monies account and some superannuation.
Mr Astor and Ms Lennon commenced living together in 2013, married in 2016, and separated for the final time in March 2022. The relationship was therefore about eight and a half years in length. Ms Lennon claimed that the parties separated for a few months in 2015–16, but it was conceded on her behalf that any such separation would not affect the decision which I need to make.
Both Ms Lennon and Mr Astor are 41. Ms Lennon works as an educator. Currently, Mr Astor is unemployed and does not pay any child support for X.
The proceedings were transferred to Division 1 on the morning of the final hearing in circumstances where the matter would not be reached in Division 2 and where I had capacity to hear the proceedings. I was told that the parties had not attended a conference as contemplated by s 79AA(9)(a) of the Act. Oddly, that is a requirement where proceedings are in Division 1, but not where they are in Division 2. I am satisfied that the circumstances in which the matter came before me are sufficiently special to justify me making final orders notwithstanding the absence of compliance with s 79AA(9)(a). It is clearly in the interests of justice – given the proceedings commenced in 2022 and the availability of judicial resources – that the matter is determined.
At the commencement of the final hearing, there were significant issues in dispute between Mr Astor and Ms Lennon. Most prominent was a dispute about whether Ms Lennon is the beneficial owner of the Suburb C apartment which was purchased in her sole name prior to the commencement of the relationship and which now has an agreed value of $1.225 million. It is not contested that Ms Lennon’s parents – Ms D and Mr E – contributed all the funds to enable the purchase of the Suburb C property. Ms Lennon’s case was that she owns the Suburb C apartment on trust for her parents. By the conclusion of the final hearing, Ms Lennon had abandoned that argument, and the issue became how her contribution of that property should affect the adjustment of the parties’ property interests, in circumstances where Mr Astor also owned a property at the commencement of the relationship, but where that property had been sold in 2014.
The parties also reached agreement as to the initial and current values of the Suburb C property (which had been in dispute at the commencement of the final hearing). They agreed that Ms Lennon would retain the Suburb C property, and each would retain their superannuation. The only issue was how the joint funds held in a controlled monies account should be distributed and whether Ms Lennon should make an additional cash payment to Mr Astor.
In closing submissions, Mr Astor sought that the parties’ property be divided as to 40 per cent to him in recognition of the greater initial contribution by Ms Lennon by way of the Suburb C property. Appropriately, given the significant differences in the values of the properties each brought in, counsel for Mr Astor abandoned the written submission contained in the Case Outline filed on his behalf that “overall the contribution based entitlement would be determined as equal in circumstances where both parties bought [sic] in a property”.
Ms Lennon sought that the matter be approached on a “two pool” basis – with the Suburb C property in one pool to which she solely contributed and the balance in the other. In the event a “one pool” approach was adopted, she sought that the parties’ property be divided as to 75 per cent to her and 25 per cent to Mr Astor (70 per cent to her for contributions and a 5 per cent adjustment).
In closing submissions, it became clear that the issues I now need to resolve are only the following:
(1) Should the parties’ contributions be assessed on a “one pool” or “two pool” basis?

(2) It having been conceded on behalf of Mr Astor and Ms Lennon that their respective contributions were otherwise equal, how should the parties’ contributions each of a property in their sole name be weighed and the parties’ overall contributions assessed?

(3) What findings should I make in respect of payments made to Ms Lennon’s father on 30 September and 1 October 2021 totalling $200,000 from the proceeds of sale of a property at Suburb F, and how, if at all, should those payments be considered in determining how the parties’ property should be adjusted?

(4) How, if at all, should I take account of the fact that Ms Lennon’s parents have been living in the Suburb C property since about 2014 (in the case of Ms D) and 2019 (in the case of Mr E)?

(5) What, if any, adjustment should I make to the contribution assessment based on the parties’ current and future financial circumstances?

The issues which I must decide are therefore quite narrow. This is notwithstanding a difficult final hearing in which the credibility of both parties was very significantly in issue. Presumably those credibility issues were a factor in the narrowing of the issues between them. It is to the parties’ (and their legal representatives’) credit that the issues were ultimately narrowed. Save for the issue of the $200,000 payment, resolution does not depend on the credibility of the parties.
For the reasons set out below, I make orders which adjust the parties’ property and superannuation interests as to 65 per cent to Ms Lennon and 35 per cent to Mr Astor.

DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

Mr Astor relied upon the following documents:
(1) His affidavit filed 22 January 2026;

(2) His Amended Financial Statement filed 21 January 2026;

(3) The affidavit of his father, Mr H, filed 4 February 2026.

Ms Lennon relied upon the following documents:
(1) Her affidavit filed 28 January 2026;

(2) Her Financial Statement filed 28 January 2026;

(3) An affidavit of her father, Mr E, filed 31 January 2026;

(4) An affidavit of her mother, Ms D, filed 2 February 2026;

Case outlines were filed on behalf of both parties.
I have read and had regard to the material listed above, and to the oral evidence given by the parties and their witnesses. I refer in these Reasons only to the evidence which is necessary for me to resolve the issues remaining in dispute.

STEPS TO BE UNDERTAKEN IN THE PROPERTY ADJUSTMENT EXERCISE

Section 79 of the Act sets out the steps which I must follow in determining an application for alteration of property interests:
(3) In considering what order (if any) should be made under this section in property settlement proceedings, the court:
(a) is to identify:
(i) the existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties to the marriage or either of them; and

(ii) the existing liabilities of the parties to the marriage or either of them; and
(b) is to take into account ... :
(i) the considerations set out in subsection (4) (considerations relating to contributions); and

(ii) the considerations set out in subsection (5) (considerations relating to current and future circumstances).

Identifying the parties’ existing property and liabilities

Tendered was a much-abbreviated Balance Sheet by which the parties agreed that the only property and liabilities I should take account of are the following:
(1) Funds held in a joint controlled monies account CBA #...99 $521,519.58

(2) B Street, Suburb C NSW [...] $1,225,000

(3) Liabilities NIL

(4) Husband’s superannuation with Super Fund 1 $240,332

(5) Wife’s superannuation with Super Fund 2 $26,326

Net total $2,013,177.58

The statutorily mandated requirement to identify the parties’ existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property and the existing liabilities of the parties appears to mean that I must do more than simply adopt that agreed Balance Sheet.
I am satisfied, based on a more detailed Balance Sheet provided by the parties and their respective Financial Statements that, in addition to the items identified above, the parties each have banks accounts with modest balances, and various debts for post-separation expenses and legal fees. I cannot attribute a value to these items on the evidence and that would, in any case, be a pointless exercise given the parties agree that these items are not to be brought into account. I am satisfied that none of the items are of such value that they would affect the justice and equity of the outcome in this matter.

Is it just and equitable to adjust the parties’ interests?

Both Ms Lennon and Mr Astor seek an adjustment of their interests in property. This was a relationship of more than eight years’ duration, during which both parties made significant contributions. The parties are no longer engaged in the joint endeavour which underpinned the organisation of their financial affairs, including how property was held during their relationship. They hold joint funds which need to be distributed between them and neither party says that they should be distributed equally. I am therefore satisfied that it is just and equitable to adjust the parties’ interests in property.

Contributions

The parties agree that, save for their respective contributions of real property, their contributions of the various kinds that are relevant were equal. Both engaged in paid work and contributed their incomes to expenses, accumulation of assets (including superannuation), and payment of debt. Both cared for X, for each other, and for their properties. Mr Astor earnt more than Ms Lennon. Ms Lennon did more parenting of X.
The issue for me is how, in assessing their respective contributions, I weigh the agreed facts that:
(1) Mr Astor brought into the relationship a property at Suburb J (“the [Suburb J] property”) which had an agreed initial net value of $200,000 and was sold in March 2014 producing net proceeds of $224,000;

(2) Ms Lennon brought into the relationship the Suburb C property which had an initial value of $850,000 and has a current value of $1.225 million. The initial value recognises that the property was effectively unencumbered from 2014 because her parents had contributed funds which fully offset the mortgage loan – those funds having been used to discharge the mortgage in 2017.

I should not assess the value of an initial contribution of an asset which is still owned by one of the parties simply by reference to either the initial or the current value of that asset (Jabour & Jabour [2019] FamCAFC 78; (2019) FLC 93-898 at [34]–[86]). The exercise is a more nuanced one than that. I must take an holistic approach to the assessment of the myriad of contributions of the parties according to the “unique facts and circumstances of the case at hand” (Preston & Preston [2022] FedCFamC1A 157; (2022) FLC 94-108 at [29]).

Should I assess the parties’ contributions on a “one pool” or “two pool” basis?

On behalf of Ms Lennon, it was said that I should assess the parties’ respective contributions by reference to “two pools”, with the Suburb C property in one pool (to which she has made 100 per cent of the contributions) and the balance in another (to which she has made 50 per cent of the contributions). The reason why I should adopt that approach was not clearly articulated. While the Suburb C property was, at least to some extent, treated by the parties as an asset which was Ms Lennon’s alone, and while one or both of her parents have lived there since 2014, the property was owned throughout the relationship, the parties lived there together for some months at the beginning of their relationship, and the property also produced rental income.
I am satisfied that it is just and equitable to adopt the usual “one pool” approach contended for on behalf of Mr Astor. Such an approach avoids the artificiality of identifying what contributions relate to which assets in circumstances where the authorities are clear that there need not be a relationship between contributions (whether financial, non-financial or to the welfare of the family) and what they produced (Dickons & Dickons (2012) 50 Fam LR 24). A “one pool” approach promotes the necessary holistic assessment.

How should the parties’ contributions each of a property in their sole name be weighed and the parties’ overall contributions assessed?

Ms Lennon’s initial contribution of the Suburb C property along with the contribution made by her parents in providing the funds first to offset the mortgage loan and then to repay it is a significant contribution.
The use to which the property was put is relevant in assessing contributions (Pierce v Pierce [1998] FamCA 74; (1999) FLC 92-844 at 85,881). Initially, the Suburb C property was a home for the parties. Ms Lennon gave evidence that, even when she and her parents have been living there, a room or rooms in the property have been rented out. She admitted that she had not declared rental income to the Australian Taxation Office. There was very little evidence about what rental income the Suburb C property produced, and how the expenses in respect of the property were paid. Ms Lennon was very evasive in giving her oral evidence about those issues. I am satisfied that the Suburb C property has produced some rental income, and that rental income has been used to pay the expenses associated with the property. Ms Lennon has managed the property.
There is no evidence that the parties made any significant improvements or renovations to the Suburb C property (Mr Astor’s evidence in relation to this point was of only the most modest of non-financial contributions by him to the property). The increase in value therefore simply reflects the increase in property prices. I infer that Ms Lennon’s ownership of the property was at least a factor in the parties being able to obtain a loan for the Suburb F property they purchased. Both in that way, and because it is still “in the pool”, the Suburb C property has been a very significant contributor to the parties’ current financial position.
Mr Astor also owned the Suburb J property at the commencement of the relationship. It had an agreed initial value of net $200,000. The net proceeds of sale of that property (being $224,000) were applied to the purchase of a vehicle and, along with some funds contributed by Ms Lennon, to the purchase of a property at Suburb G in the parties’ joint names. The Suburb G property was sold in early 2016, producing some gain for the parties.
Ms Lennon’s contributions by way of the Suburb C property were therefore significantly greater than Mr Astor’s by way of the Suburb J property, but that must be weighed with the myriad of contributions over what was nearly a nine-year relationship. Those contributions were necessarily not as significant as they would have been over a very long relationship, but were substantial nonetheless.

The relevance of the fact that Ms Lennon’s parents lived in the Suburb C property

A submission was made on behalf of Mr Astor that in assessing the weight to be given to Ms Lennon’s contribution of the Suburb C property, I should take into account that her parents have lived there “rent free” for many years (her mother since 2014 and her father since 2019). It was said that I could weigh that as part of the contribution assessment exercise (effectively to diminish the weight to be given to the contribution of the Suburb C property). This submission has some superficial attraction to it, but contributions towards extended family members are not relevant contributions under s 79(4). This matter is therefore only relevant as an aspect of what use was made of the property. Rental income has still been received notwithstanding Ms Lennon’s parents have lived there, and there is no evidence by which I can assess how much greater that income might have been if the whole property had been rented out. In those circumstances, in assessing the parties’ contributions, I attach no significance to the fact that the property was used as a home for Ms Lennon’s parents.

Conclusion in relation to contributions

Overall, I am satisfied that Ms Lennon’s contribution of the Suburb C property, weighed with Mr Astor’s initial contribution of the Suburb J property, and both parties’ contributions over the almost nine-year relationship is appropriately recognised by a division of the parties’ property as to 65 per cent to Ms Lennon and 35 per cent to Mr Astor.

Current and future financial circumstances

I turn to the considerations in s 79(5) so far as they are relevant.
Both parties are relatively young and have decades of their working lives ahead of them. There was no evidence of any health issues or like matters of relevance. Neither has re-partnered.
I must consider “the income, property and financial resources of each of the parties to the marriage and the physical and mental capacity of each of them for appropriate gainful employment” (s 79(5)(c)). Ms Lennon is working as an educator and earns approximately $77,000 per annum. Mr Astor is unemployed and in receipt of a JobSeeker allowance and Family Tax Benefit, which total approximately $500 a week. He therefore earns significantly less than Ms Lennon. Both parties live with their parents and have the advantage of sharing household expenses with their parents.
Mr Astor’s evidence about his attempts to find work fell short of any proper detail. He says he has made numerous applications – but does not detail the work which he has attempted to obtain. In those circumstances, I cannot be satisfied that he is unlikely to find paid work if he engages in appropriate efforts. In any event, he acknowledged in cross examination that his work prospects have been adversely affected by the fact that he pled guilty to an offence against Ms Lennon. The fault for that of course lies with him, and it would not be just and equitable to have regard to that matter in determining how to divide the parties’ property. There is no suggestion in the evidence that Mr Astor’s working capacity has been compromised by joint decisions made by the parties during the relationship.
Ms Lennon is to receive a greater proportion of the property because of her greater contributions and it appears likely that, even if she continues to live in the Suburb C property, she will continue to obtain some income from renting out part of the property.
There are the further relevant facts that Ms Lennon has slightly more care of X than Mr Astor (relevant under s 79(5)(f)), and that she currently receives no child support from him (relevant under s 79(5)(s)).
I am satisfied that the matters referred to above do not warrant any adjustment from the contribution-based assessment.
I have considered whether I should take into account two additional matters affecting the parties’ current and future financial circumstances pursuant to s 79(5)(v) (“any other fact or circumstance which, in the opinion of the court, the justice of the case requires to be taken into account”): first, the fact that Ms Lennon’s parents have lived in the Suburb C property for a total of more than 10 years; second, that the parties transferred $200,000 to Mr E in September and October 2021 after the sale of the Suburb F property owned by the parties.
In relation to the former, I have determined that justice and equity does not require me to take account of an arrangement made in the context of the reciprocity and mutual benefit involved in an extended family. It is not contested, for example, that Ms D and Mr E have assisted significantly in the care of X. They provided the original funds for the Suburb C property. The parties have continued to benefit from rental income, notwithstanding that the property has been, and continues to be, home to Ms D and Mr E.
In relation to the $200,000 payment, I am satisfied that the money transferred to Mr E was pursuant to an agreement between the parties and Mr E as to the funding of an investment in the Suburb F property and as to repayment of funds lent to them for the purchase of a car. Mr Astor gave evidence that he had transferred the money with the intention to benefit X and into what he thought was an account controlled by Ms Lennon on X’s behalf. That was frankly unbelievable evidence, particularly when seen in light of the following:
(1) The transactions, while including the reference “transfer to [X]”, were labelled “Commbank app home and car loan”.

(2) By way of disclosure, Mr Astor sent screenshots of the transactions to Ms Lennon’s solicitors. I am satisfied he redacted the description “Commbank app home and car loan” because he understood that it was inconsistent with his case;

(3) Mr E gave detailed and unchallenged evidence about the agreement pursuant to which he was repaid;

(4) It is inherently unlikely that parents with these parties’ means would put aside $200,000 for their infant daughter; and

(5) My general assessment of Mr Astor’s credibility, including but not limited to the facts that he has pled guilty to an offence against Ms Lennon and that he admitted he lied to me under oath about his attempts to discuss his evidence with his lawyers whilst under cross examination and despite having been warned by me not to do so.

No adjustment pursuant to s 79(5) is just and equitable, neither by virtue of these two matters nor for the other matters referred to above.

JUST AND EQUITABLE OUTCOME AND ORDERS

Orders will be made adjusting the parties’ property interests as to 35 per cent to Mr Astor and 65 per cent to Ms Lennon, reflecting my assessment of their contributions and my determination that there should be no further adjustment. Given their agreement as to which assets they should respectively keep, this requires that Mr Astor receives from the controlled monies account the sum of $464,280.15 and retains his superannuation at $240,332. Ms Lennon will retain the Suburb C property, the balance of the funds in the controlled monies account ($57,239.43), and her superannuation at $26,326. There may be some additional interest in the controlled monies account, and that will be divided to effect the overall percentages which I have determined are just and equitable.
Ms Lennon will retain a home to live in and/or from which she may earn some rental income, but only a very small amount of superannuation. She has a long working life ahead of her. Mr Astor will have a significant sum towards his own housing, in the event he determines to live independently, and will retain his significantly greater superannuation. He did not seek that the outcome include a split of his superannuation and so, while he will receive proportionately more superannuation and less by way of immediately accessible assets than Ms Lennon, I am satisfied that this outcome is just and equitable. The making of orders as sought by Mr Astor in relation to the personal property to be retained by the parties, indemnities, the requirement on the parties to do all acts and things to give effect to the orders and an order pursuant to s 106A of the Act was not opposed on behalf of Ms Lennon, and I make those orders.
